text: Bel; perhaps Nimrod, (Calmet) or Saturn, to whom they sacrificed their children. (Worthington)<hr>Nabo, "the oracle" of Belus. The Chaldeans adored statues and beasts. But the Persians worshipped the elements. (Calmet)<hr>Xerxes destroyed the tomb of Belus, after his expedition into Greece. (Arrian vii.) He had there demolished the temples, (Herodotus 8:109.) pretending (Haydock) that "the world is the house of the gods." (Cicero, Leg. ii.)<hr>Weariness. The priests affected to be weighed down, as if the god were present. (Baruch 6:25.) (St. Cyril)

They. Literally, "their soul." (Haydock)<hr>The pagans must have supposed they had one. (Calmet)<hr>Captivity. "There are as many triumphs over the gods as over men." (Tertullian)<hr>The former shared the fate of their adorers. Their statues were plundered.
By my. Vulgate may have read a me ab, (Calmet) instead of a meo. Hebrew and Septuagint, "taken from the womb," (Haydock) and treated with the utmost tenderness. (Calmet)

Bird; Cyrus, whose rapid conquests are thus denoted. (Calmet)<hr>He chose a golden eagle, with wings expanded, for his standard. (Xenophon vii.)<hr>Christ came from heaven to redeem the world, Psalm 18:6., and Malachias 4:2. (Calmet)<hr>He was the orient, adored by the eastern sages, to whom the prophet refers. (St. Jerome) (Worthington)

Israel. It shall no longer be a reproach. Cyrus shall restore my people to their own country. But Christ more full accomplished what is here declared respecting the establishment of his Church. (Calmet)
